prompt: Why do coffee shops so often have similar aesthetics (exposed brick, pendant lights, light wood) across very different cities? Offer a serious analysis, not a joke.
response:
The sameness is not an accident of taste; it is the predictable outcome of a global industry that has converged on a single business model and a single customer segment.

1. A shared business model  
   The dominant independent-plus-chain coffee shop is a “third place” that must signal both authenticity and scalability. Authenticity justifies a $4–$6 price for a beverage whose raw ingredients cost pennies; scalability allows the formula to be reproduced in new neighborhoods and new countries. Exposed brick, pendant lights, and pale wood are the cheapest, most portable way to broadcast “authentic craft” while remaining modular and replicable.

2. A shared customer segment  
   The core customer is the laptop-carrying knowledge worker who is willing to pay for a place that feels more “designed” than home but less corporate than an office. This demographic—urban, college-educated, globally mobile—has the same aesthetic expectations whether it is in Brooklyn, Berlin, or Bangkok. When landlords, investors, and franchisees all chase the same psychographic, the visual toolkit converges.

3. Supply-chain determinism  
   Global vendors now sell turnkey café packages: Edison-bulb pendants from Guangzhou, reclaimed-wood tables from Lithuania, hexagonal floor tiles from Spain. Because these items are available in the same catalogs everywhere, the marginal cost of adopting the “look” is lower than inventing a new one. Once a critical mass of cafés adopts the package, it becomes the default reference point for landlords, permitting departments, and even city design guidelines.

4. Platform aesthetics  
   Instagram and Yelp compress local variation into a single visual feed. A café that deviates too far from the recognizable palette risks being un-photogenic and therefore un-discoverable. Over time, the most successful posts reinforce the palette, creating a feedback loop in which owners design for the feed first and the neighborhood second.

5. Regulatory and economic constraints  
   Exposed brick and pendant lights are not just fashionable; they are practical. Brick walls often cannot be altered in older buildings with historic-preservation overlays; leaving them bare is cheaper than drywalling. Pendant lighting allows high ceilings to feel intimate without the cost of recessed fixtures. Light woods (birch, maple, pine) are inexpensive, easy to mill, and lighten spaces that might otherwise feel cramped. The aesthetic is therefore as much a response to zoning, labor, and material costs as to taste.

6. Cultural flattening through franchising  
   Even when a café is technically independent, it is often financed or advised by the same accelerator funds that have already refined the formula in other cities. The “look” is bundled with the menu engineering, the POS system, and the supplier contracts. Deviating from the package increases perceived risk for investors and delays permitting because non-standard plans require extra review.

In short, the coffee-shop interior is not a neutral backdrop; it is a highly optimized compromise between the economics of global
